value_raw: Legal drinking age 18 to purchase, no statutory minimum for private consumption; rakia (grape brandy, ~50% ABV) is the cultural anchor
notes: Same cultural root/role as Albania's raki. Real hard line: drink-driving tolerance effectively zero, 0.05 BAC can trigger a fine

value_raw: Not applicable — no leave-and-return loophole exists inside Schengen
notes: The 90/180 clock is shared across the whole Schengen zone; leaving Bulgaria for another Schengen country doesn't reset anything

value_raw: Adopted euro 1 Jan 2026; cabinet resigned Jan 2026 after mass protests (peaked ~1 Dec, 50,000-100,000 people, Gen Z/TikTok-organized) against a budget proposal
notes: Largest demonstrations since the 2020-2021 anti-corruption wave; triggered Bulgaria's 8th parliamentary election in 5 years (April 19, 2026)

value_raw: Class A (high-risk) drug, grouped with heroin/cocaine/MDMA; 1-6 years imprisonment + 2,000-10,000 leva fines for standard possession
notes: No legal recreational market, no tolerated home-grow threshold, no meaningful medical-cannabis patient access. A genuinely harder-line posture than Albania's (which has an administrative-fine off-ramp) — no strong near-term legalization signal

value_raw: 2nd-most-corrupt EU member state (Transparency International)
notes: The new government's own mandate is explicitly built on addressing this — current and serious enough to have driven the largest protests in years

fact_label: Digital Nomad Permit — eligible income sources
value_raw: Remote employee of a non-EU/EEA/Swiss company; 25%+ equity owner of a foreign-registered business; OR freelancer w/ 1+ year non-Bulgarian client history
notes: Income must be foreign-sourced — no Bulgarian-employer or Bulgarian-client income counts

value_raw: Type D visa first (4-8 weeks), then the permit itself within 14 days of arrival; 1 year, renewable
notes: Genuinely comparable in spirit to Portugal's D8 visa

fact_label: Digital Nomad/Freelancer Residence Permit — income threshold
notes: Applications opened 20 Dec 2025, 2026 is the program's first full year. A meaningfully higher bar than Albania's unsettled ~$9,800/year figure — a real, load-bearing difference between this project's two newest candidates. Sourced via mainstream coverage (Euronews, CNBC), among the better-sourced findings in either country's file

value_raw: Many house/villa listings include land even when marketed simply as a house
notes: Named across multiple sources as the most common costly mistake non-EU buyers make.

Broadly in line with Western Europe, far below Baltic or Balkan extremes

fact_label: National Health Insurance Fund (NHIF) access route — EU vs. non-EU
value_raw: EU citizens: employment enrollment, self-insurance, or an S1-form transfer from home country. Non-EU nationals: no equivalent eased door — private health insurance is the real day-one mechanism
notes: Same shape as this file's land-ownership finding above — Bulgaria's EU membership does not transfer an eased door to a non-EU foreign national on its own

fact_label: National Health Insurance Fund (NHIF) contribution obligation — timing for non-EU residents
value_raw: Attaches once a long-term or permanent residence permit is held (~5 years continuous residence, per the standard residence ladder)
notes: A real multi-year gap during which private insurance, not the National Health Insurance Fund (NHIF), is the non-EU resident's actual coverage mechanism

value_raw: No separate health certificate/permit needed — just a valid EU Pet Passport with current rabies vaccination and readable microchip
notes: An eighth exception to this project's dog-only pet-import gap. Standard EU pet-passport regime

fact_label: Pet import (dog and cat) — non-EU origin
value_raw: Microchip, rabies vaccination, official vet health certificate, PLUS an origin-dependent rabies antibody titre test
notes: Unlike Albania (titer confirmed NOT required from any origin), Bulgaria's titer requirement is origin-dependent, not uniformly waived — whether it applies to a US-origin pet specifically is unresolved. No quarantine if requirements met precisely, but incomplete documentation can trigger it

fact_label: Private health insurance — base cost (younger applicant)
notes: Basic plans for ages 25-35; premiums rise with age and pre-existing conditions, no Bulgaria-specific age-tiered cost table found

value_raw: Modern, affordable, English-speaking; the strongly preferred route for expats nationally

value_raw: Underfunded — ~4-5% of GDP, among the lowest in the EU; poor facility/hygiene standards, understaffing, waiting times weeks-to-months outside major cities
notes: OECD's own 2026 country health-system review — corroborated by multiple expat-healthcare guides describing corruption/underfunding as having eroded the system's reputation
